serving.natural_language_portion: One appetiser serving of Thai mango salad is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a small shared plate or individual starter portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mango salad, Thai-style (composite estimate from raw mango, lime juice, fish sauce, chili, shallots, dried shrimp, peanuts, palm sugar)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Thai mango salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for raw green mango (FDC ID 169910), lime juice (FDC ID 167747), fish sauce (FDC ID 175148), roasted peanuts (FDC ID 172430), dried shrimp (FDC ID 175180), shallots (FDC ID 170498), and palm sugar (FDC ID 168878). Typical recipe proportions applied: green mango 50%, vegetables/shallots/chili 15%, dressing (lime juice, fish sauce, palm sugar) 20%, dried shrimp 5%, roasted peanuts 10%. Values represent per-100 g of finished salad.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai mango salad as a composite dish. GI estimated at 51 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being raw green mango (GI approximately 51, per University of Sydney GI Database entry for mango, Mangifera indica). The dressing components (lime juice, fish sauce) and protein/fat from peanuts and dried shrimp further attenuate the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (51 × 21.75 g) / 100 ≈ 11.1. Available carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 14.5 × 1.5 = 21.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Thai mango salad contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (630 mg per 150 g serving) primarily from fish sauce; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or substitute with low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from mango sugars and palm sugar dressing
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 51 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 11 per 150 g serving, Thai mango salad is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; however, the palm sugar and ripe mango content contribute free sugars (~15 g per serving) that require carbohydrate counting and portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts, high vitamin C
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thai mango salad is relatively low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g) with beneficial unsaturated fats from roasted peanuts, and provides vitamin C (~22 mg per 100 g) and potassium (~185 mg per 100 g); the primary cardiovascular concern is the sodium load from fish sauce, which should be moderated in patients with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Mango Salad (ยำมะม่วง, Yam Mamuang) is a vibrant Southeast Asian appetiser originating from Thailand, widely served across Singapore and Malaysia in Thai restaurants and Zi Char establishments. The dish is built around shredded or julienned green (unripe) mango, which provides a firm, crunchy texture and a sharp, tart flavour profile. The salad is dressed with a balance of fish sauce, fresh lime juice, and palm sugar, creating the characteristic Thai flavour triad of salty, sour, and sweet. Dried shrimp add umami depth and a subtle seafood note, while roasted peanuts contribute crunch and richness. Fresh chili and shallots provide heat and aromatic pungency. The dish is served cold or at room temperature and is typically presented as a shared starter. Nutritionally, it is moderately low in calories (~98 kcal per 100 g), low in saturated fat, and provides useful amounts of vitamin C and potassium from the mango. The glycemic index is estimated at 51 (low), making it a relatively glycemia-friendly option, though the palm sugar and mango sugars contribute meaningful free sugars (~10 g per 100 g). Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains peanut, shellfish (dried shrimp), and fish allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its traditional form.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Thai mango salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients. GI was estimated by analogy to raw green mango from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no direct composite dish entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA for Thai mango salad. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source entries for the constituent ingredients of this dish and is not expected to be a meaningful contributor. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the constituent ingredient entries in USDA FoodData Central for this dish composition. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in the traditional preparation of this dish; confirmed negligible per USDA ingredient data. 5. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate based on raw green mango as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor; the actual composite GI may be lower due to fat, protein, and acid content of the dressing attenuating glycemic response. 6. The input record tagged this dish under 'Malaysian' cuisine and 'Crab / Zi Char' cuisine tags; the dish is correctly classified as Thai in origin, though it is commonly served in Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char and Thai restaurants. 7. Halal status is marked false as traditional preparation uses fish sauce (typically halal-certifiable but not universally certified) and dried shrimp; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed. 8. The serving size of 150 g is an estimate for a restaurant appetiser portion; actual portion sizes vary by establishment.
